questions.stem: Which sentence uses the apostrophe correctly to show possession?
options: The girls bag was left on the bus.
The girl's bag was left on the bus.
The girls bag's was left on the bus.
The girls' bag's were left on the bus.
questions.explanation: To show that a single owner possesses something, add an apostrophe and then "s" to the owner. One girl owns the bag, so "girl's" is correct. The other options either drop the apostrophe or add it to "bags," which is a plain plural, not a possessive.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correctly punctuated sentence.
options: We bought apples oranges and bananas.
We bought apples, oranges and bananas.
We, bought apples oranges and bananas.
We bought, apples oranges, and bananas.
questions.explanation: Items in a simple series are separated by commas. The first option correctly separates "apples" and "oranges" with a comma before the final "and." The other options either omit the needed commas or insert commas where no break belongs, such as between the verb and its object.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly uses a semicolon to join two related independent clauses?
options: The rain stopped; the sun came out.
The rain stopped; and the sun came out.
The rain stopped, the sun came out.
The rain stopped the sun; came out.
questions.explanation: A semicolon joins two closely related independent clauses without a conjunction. The second option links two complete sentences correctly. Putting "and" after the semicolon is wrong because a semicolon already does the joining, and a plain comma between two independent clauses creates a comma splice.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Where should a colon be placed in this sentence? "She packed three items a torch a map and a flask."
options: After "map"
After "packed"
After "torch"
After "items"
questions.explanation: A colon introduces a list after a complete independent clause. "She packed three items" is a complete clause, and the list that follows explains the items, so the colon belongs after "items." Placing it earlier would break the clause before it is complete.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence uses commas correctly to set off a nonessential clause?
options: My brother, who lives in Accra, is a doctor.
My brother who lives in Accra is a doctor.
My brother, who lives in Accra is a doctor.
My brother who lives in Accra, is a doctor.
questions.explanation: A nonessential clause adds extra information and is set off by a pair of commas, one before and one after. The second option correctly places commas on both sides of "who lives in Accra." Using only one comma leaves the clause half enclosed, which is incorrect.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the sentence with the correct use of the apostrophe in a contraction.
options: Its going to rain, later today.
Its' going to rain later today.
It's going to rain later today.
Its going to rain later today.
questions.explanation: The contraction of "it is" is "it's," with the apostrophe replacing the missing letter. The sentence means "it is going to rain," so "it's" is correct. The form "its" without an apostrophe is the possessive, as in "its tail," and does not fit here.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly punctuates a direct quotation?
options: She said, I will come tomorrow.
She said "I will come tomorrow".
She said, "I will come tomorrow."
She said "I will come tomorrow."
questions.explanation: A direct quotation is introduced by a comma after the reporting verb and is enclosed in quotation marks, with the closing punctuation placed inside the closing mark. The second option follows all three rules. The others drop the introducing comma, omit the quotation marks, or put the full stop outside the marks.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence shows correct plural possession?
options: The student's results were published, for all of them.
The students results were published.
The students's results were published.
The students' results were published.
questions.explanation: To show possession by a plural noun that already ends in "s," add only an apostrophe after the "s." Many students own the results, so "students'" is correct. Adding another "s" after the apostrophe or leaving the apostrophe out is wrong for a regular plural.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the sentence that correctly uses a comma after an introductory element.
options: After, the meeting we went home.
After the meeting we, went home.
After the meeting, we went home.
After the meeting we went home.
questions.explanation: A comma follows an introductory phrase to separate it from the main clause. The phrase "After the meeting" introduces the sentence, so a comma belongs right after it. The other placements break the phrase or split the subject from its verb.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly uses a question mark?
options: He asked whether I was ready?
Are you ready to begin?
Are you ready to begin.
Are you ready to begin
questions.explanation: A question mark ends a direct question. "Are you ready to begin?" is a direct question and takes a question mark. The first option is an indirect question reported as a statement, so it should end with a full stop, not a question mark.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence places the apostrophe correctly for an irregular plural?
options: The children's toys were everywhere.
The childrens' toys were everywhere.
The childrens toys were everywhere.
The children's toy's were everywhere.
questions.explanation: An irregular plural that does not end in "s," such as "children," forms the possessive by adding an apostrophe and "s." So "children's" is correct. Putting the apostrophe after a wrongly added "s" or omitting it altogether is incorrect.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the correctly punctuated sentence with a conjunctive adverb joining two clauses.
options: I studied hard however; I failed the test.
I studied hard; however, I failed the test.
I studied hard however I failed the test.
I studied hard, however I failed the test.
questions.explanation: When a conjunctive adverb such as "however" joins two independent clauses, a semicolon comes before it and a comma after it. The second option follows this pattern correctly. Using only a comma before "however" creates a comma splice.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly uses commas to set off a word of direct address?
options: Kofi please, close the door.
Kofi please close the door.
Kofi, please close the door.
Kofi please close, the door.
questions.explanation: When you name the person you are speaking to, that name is set off by a comma. "Kofi" is the person being addressed at the start of the sentence, so a comma follows it. The other placements separate words that belong together.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the sentence that correctly uses a colon to introduce an explanation.
options: There was one problem: the car would not start.
There was one problem, the car: would not start.
There was one problem the car would not start.
There was: one problem the car would not start.
questions.explanation: A colon can introduce an explanation or example after a complete independent clause. "There was one problem" is complete, and what follows explains the problem, so the colon is correctly placed. The other options omit the colon or insert it before the clause is complete.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly avoids a comma splice?
options: The bell rang the pupils went home.
The bell rang, the pupils went home.
The bell rang, so the pupils went home.
The bell rang, then, the pupils went home.
questions.explanation: Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone, which is a comma splice. Adding the coordinating conjunction "so" after the comma joins them correctly. The third option runs the clauses together with no punctuation, which is a fused sentence.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence uses the apostrophe correctly?
options: The dog wagged it tail happily.
The dog wagged its' tail happily.
The dog wagged its tail happily.
The dog wagged it's tail happily.
questions.explanation: The possessive form of "it" is "its," with no apostrophe. The dog owns the tail, so "its" is correct. The form "it's" means "it is" and would wrongly read as "the dog wagged it is tail."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the sentence that correctly separates two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction.
options: The film was, long but it held our attention.
The film was long, but it held our attention.
The film was long but it held our attention.
The film was long but, it held our attention.
questions.explanation: When a coordinating conjunction such as "but" joins two independent clauses, a comma comes before the conjunction. The second option places the comma correctly before "but." The other options either omit it or misplace it after the conjunction or inside the first clause.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly uses an exclamation mark?
options: What, a wonderful surprise
What a wonderful surprise.
What a wonderful surprise!
What a wonderful surprise?
questions.explanation: An exclamation mark ends a sentence that expresses strong feeling. "What a wonderful surprise!" shows excitement and so takes an exclamation mark. A full stop would underplay the emotion, and a question mark wrongly turns it into a question.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly punctuates the date with commas?
options: She was born on June 5, 2004, in Kumasi.
She was born on June 5 2004, in Kumasi.
She was born on June 5 2004 in Kumasi.
She was born on June, 5 2004 in Kumasi.
questions.explanation: In the month, day, year format, a comma separates the day from the year, and another comma follows the year when the sentence continues. The second option places both commas correctly. The comma never goes between the month and the day.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the sentence that correctly uses a hyphen in a compound adjective before a noun.
options: She gave a well-written essay.
She gave a, well written essay.
She gave a well written, essay.
She gave a well written essay.
questions.explanation: When two words act together as a single adjective before a noun, they are joined by a hyphen. "Well-written" describes the essay and so takes a hyphen. Without the hyphen the two words are read separately, which weakens the link before the noun.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence correctly uses commas around a parenthetical phrase?
options: The result, in my opinion was unfair.
The result in my opinion, was unfair.
The result, in my opinion, was unfair.
The result in, my opinion, was unfair.
questions.explanation: A parenthetical phrase that interrupts a sentence is enclosed by a comma on each side. "In my opinion" is such an interruption, so it takes a comma before and after it. Using only one comma leaves the phrase incompletely enclosed.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence uses quotation marks correctly for a title within a sentence?
options: We read the poem The Road Not Taken in class.
We read the poem, "The Road Not Taken in class".
We read the poem "The Road Not Taken" in class.
We read the poem "The Road Not Taken in class."
questions.explanation: Quotation marks enclose the title of a short work such as a poem, and only the title itself goes inside the marks. The first option encloses exactly the title "The Road Not Taken" and leaves the rest of the sentence outside, which is correct. The third and fourth options wrongly pull "in class" inside the quotation marks.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the sentence in which the comma correctly separates coordinate adjectives.
options: It was a long tiring, journey.
It was a long tiring journey,
It was a, long tiring journey.
It was a long, tiring journey.
questions.explanation: When two adjectives independently modify the same noun and could be joined by "and," a comma separates them. "Long" and "tiring" both describe the journey, and we could say "long and tiring," so a comma belongs between them. The other placements separate words that should not be parted.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence ends correctly with a single full stop?
options: The lecture finished early today
The lecture finished early today..
The lecture finished early today.
The lecture, finished early today
questions.explanation: A declarative sentence ends with exactly one full stop. The second option closes the statement with a single full stop, which is correct. The first omits the full stop, the third doubles it, and the fourth both lacks the end stop and inserts a comma that wrongly splits the subject from its verb.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide
